questions.question: Explain why a polynomial of degree 3 cannot have more than three zeroes.
questions.answer: Because the maximum number of zeroes of a polynomial is equal to its degree.

questions.question: Why is x² + 1 = 0 said to have no real zeroes?
questions.answer: Because x² is always non-negative and cannot be equal to −1 for any real x.

questions.question: Explain why the degree of the zero polynomial is not defined.
questions.answer: Because it has no non-zero term to determine the highest power of the variable.

questions.question: How does the graph of a polynomial help in finding its zeroes?
questions.answer: The x-coordinates where the graph intersects the x-axis give the zeroes of the polynomial.

questions.question: Explain why a constant polynomial generally has no zeroes.
questions.answer: Because its graph is a horizontal line that does not intersect the x-axis.

questions.question: Justify that a linear polynomial has at most one zero.
questions.answer: Because its graph is a straight line that can intersect the x-axis at only one point.

questions.question: Why does p(0) give the constant term of a polynomial?
questions.answer: Because all terms containing x become zero when x = 0.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between the number of zeroes and the degree of a polynomial.
questions.answer: The maximum number of zeroes a polynomial can have is equal to its degree.

questions.question: Why is x⁻² + 3 not a polynomial?
questions.answer: Because the power of x is negative, which is not allowed in a polynomial.

questions.question: Explain why the graph of a quadratic polynomial is a parabola.
questions.answer: Because the highest power of the variable is 2, which gives a parabolic curve.

questions.question: Why can a polynomial have fewer zeroes than its degree?
questions.answer: Because some zeroes may be repeated or not real.

questions.question: Explain why the x-intercepts of a polynomial graph represent its zeroes.
questions.answer: At x-intercepts, the value of y is zero, which means the polynomial becomes zero.

questions.question: Why does a polynomial with even degree open upwards or downwards?
questions.answer: Because the leading coefficient determines the direction of the graph.

questions.question: Explain why the polynomial x³ always passes through the origin.
questions.answer: Because p(0) = 0, so the graph passes through the origin.

questions.question: Why is the standard form of a polynomial useful?
questions.answer: Because it helps in identifying degree, coefficients and type of polynomial easily.

questions.question: Explain why graphical method gives only approximate zeroes.
questions.answer: Because the accuracy depends on the scale and precision of the graph.

questions.question: Why is a polynomial considered a continuous function?
questions.answer: Because its graph has no breaks or gaps.

questions.question: Explain why x² − 4 has two zeroes.
questions.answer: Because its graph cuts the x-axis at two points.

questions.question: Why does the polynomial x² never have more than two zeroes?
questions.answer: Because its degree is two.

questions.question: Explain why polynomials are useful in real-life problems.
questions.answer: Because they model smooth and predictable relationships between quantities.

questions.question: Why does changing the coefficient of the highest power affect the graph shape?
questions.answer: Because it controls the steepness and direction of the graph.

questions.question: Explain why x = −2 is a zero of x² − 4.
questions.answer: Substituting x = −2 gives 0, so it is a zero.

questions.question: Why are fractional powers not allowed in polynomials?
questions.answer: Because polynomial exponents must be non-negative integers.

questions.question: Explain why the polynomial x² + x + 1 has no real zeroes.
questions.answer: Because its graph does not intersect the x-axis.

questions.question: Why is the concept of degree important in polynomials?
questions.answer: Because it determines the number of zeroes and the shape of the graph.
